content.text: Azure organizes resources into a four-level management hierarchy: Management Groups, Subscriptions, Resource Groups, and Resources. Each level is a scope for applying Azure Policy, RBAC role assignments, and (for resource groups and resources) resource locks. Policies and RBAC assigned at a higher scope are inherited by all child scopes. This hierarchy enables enterprises to apply consistent governance policies across hundreds of subscriptions without configuring each one individually.

attrs.alt: Azure management scope hierarchy: Management Groups, Subscriptions, Resource Groups, Resources

content.text: Management groups sit above subscriptions in the hierarchy. An Azure tenant has a single root management group that contains all other management groups and subscriptions. Organizations create a tree of management groups that mirrors their organizational or governance structure: for example, a root management group with 'Production' and 'Non-Production' child management groups, where 'Production' contains 'Finance', 'HR', and 'Engineering' child groups, each corresponding to a set of subscriptions. Management groups support up to 6 levels of depth (not counting root) and up to 10,000 management groups per tenant.

policies assigned at a management group automatically apply to all subscriptions and resource groups under that group. This is the primary reason to use management groups — applying security baselines and compliance controls across an entire organizational unit with a single assignment.

role assignments at a management group scope grant access to all resources in all child subscriptions. Useful for cross-subscription roles like security auditors (Reader on the root MG) or billing readers.

the implicit parent of all subscriptions in the tenant. Policies assigned here apply to every subscription. Global deny policies (e.g., block specific Azure regions) are typically assigned at the root.
a single management group can have up to 10,000 subscriptions as direct children.

# Management Groups: organize subscriptions and apply policies at scale

# List management groups in the tenant
az account management-group list \
  --query "[].{name:name, displayName:displayName, parent:parent.id}" \
  --output table

# Create a management group
az account management-group create \
  --name production \
  --display-name 'Production Workloads'

# Create a child management group under 'production'
az account management-group create \
  --name finance-prod \
  --display-name 'Finance Production' \
  --parent production

# Move a subscription into a management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name finance-prod \
  --subscription <subscriptionId>

# Assign an Azure Policy at the management group scope (applies to all child subscriptions)
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-tags-on-rgs \
  --display-name 'Require cost-center tag on resource groups' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/<policyId>' \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/production

# Assign an RBAC role at the management group scope
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee auditors@contoso.com \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/production

A subscription is a billing unit and a trust boundary with Entra ID. All resources in a subscription share a billing account, usage quotas, and a single Azure AD tenant for authentication. Subscriptions are the unit of isolation for cost management and for applying different policies to different environments (production vs. development). Large enterprises typically use separate subscriptions per environment (prod, non-prod), per business unit, or per Azure region, rather than using a single subscription for everything.
Simple orgs; separate prod from dev/test; different cost owners
Finance, HR, Engineering teams with separate cost allocation and RBAC admin
Strict isolation between applications; simplifies decommissioning
Shared services: Hub VNet, Shared DNS, Log Analytics workspace, Azure Firewall
Developer experimentation with permissive policies; budget cap applied

# Subscription: billing unit and security boundary

# List subscriptions available to the logged-in identity
az account list \
  --query "[].{name:name, id:id, state:state, tenantId:tenantId}" \
  --output table

# Switch to a specific subscription
az account set --subscription <subscriptionId>

# View subscription-level RBAC assignments
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --query "[?!contains(scope, 'resourceGroups')].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  --output table

# Assign a subscription-level budget alert
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name monthly-spend-alert \
  --category Cost \
  --amount 10000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-07-01 \
  --end-date 2027-06-30 \
  --resource-group-filter myRG \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["finance@contoso.com"]}]'

# Check subscription limits (quota)
az vm list-usage \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[?currentValue > 0].{name:name.localizedValue, current:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

A resource group is a logical container for related Azure resources. Resources in a resource group share a lifecycle — when you delete a resource group, all resources inside are deleted. Resource groups are the primary unit for deploying and managing application environments. A common pattern is one resource group per application tier (myapp-web, myapp-data, myapp-shared) or one resource group per environment (myapp-prod, myapp-staging, myapp-dev). Resource groups are always in a specific Azure region, but resources inside can be in any region.
group resources that are created and deleted together. A resource group for a dev environment can be deleted entirely when the environment is no longer needed.
assign roles at the resource group scope to grant access to a set of related resources without granting access to the whole subscription.
ARM templates, Bicep files, and Terraform modules deploy to a specific resource group. Deployment history is tracked per resource group (last 800 deployments).
tags applied to a resource group are not automatically applied to its resources in billing (resources must be individually tagged or inherit via Azure Policy DeployIfNotExists).

# Resource Groups: lifecycle and access control boundary

# Create a resource group with mandatory tags
az group create \
  --name myRG-finance-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCenter=Finance Team=FinanceEng

# List resource groups with their tags
az group list \
  --query "[].{name:name, location:location, environment:tags.Environment, costCenter:tags.CostCenter}" \
  --output table

# Assign RBAC at resource group scope (narrower than subscription)
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee finance-dev-team@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG-finance-prod

# Apply a resource lock to prevent deletion (CanNotDelete or ReadOnly)
az lock create \
  --name ProductionLock \
  --resource-group myRG-finance-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --notes 'Production environment — requires change management to delete'

# Move resources between resource groups (same region and subscription)
az resource move \
  --destination-group myRG-finance-dr \
  --ids $(az resource list -g myRG-finance-prod --query '[?type=="Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts"].id' -o tsv)

# Delete entire resource group (deletes all resources inside — use with care)
az group delete \
  --name myRG-dev-temp \
  --yes \
  --no-wait

The individual resource is the most granular scope for RBAC and locks. Resource-level role assignments override inheritance only in the sense that a deny assignment at the resource level blocks access even if a higher scope grants it. In practice, resource-level assignments are used when one external party needs access to a specific resource (not the whole resource group) or when one resource requires a stricter lock than the rest of the group.

# Resource-level scope: finest-grained RBAC and lock

# Assign RBAC at individual resource scope (overrides group-level if needed)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --assignee external-partner@partner.com \
  --scope $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv)

# Apply a resource-level lock (stronger than resource group lock)
az lock create \
  --name db-lock \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource-name myProdDB \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Sql/servers/databases \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --parent mySQLServer

# View all locks on a resource group and its resources
az lock list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:properties.level, scope:id}" \
  --output table

# Tag an individual resource (not at group level)
az resource tag \
  --ids $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --tags CostCenter=Finance Owner=alice@contoso.com Tier=Web

# Query resources across all resource groups by tag
az resource list \
  --tag CostCenter=Finance \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:type, rg:resourceGroup}" \
  --output table

The inheritance model is top-down: assignments at a higher scope apply to all lower scopes automatically. Azure Policy supports policy exemptions that allow specific child scopes to opt out of an inherited policy with an optional expiry date. RBAC deny assignments (not role assignments) block access at a specific scope even if a parent scope grants it — deny assignments are rare and typically used for break-glass scenarios or external contractor isolation.

# Policies and RBAC inherit from parent scopes to child scopes

# Scenario: assign a 'deny public storage accounts' policy at Management Group
# It will automatically apply to all subscriptions and resource groups under that MG

az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-public-storage \
  --policy deny-public-blob-access-definition \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/production

# Exclusion: exempt one subscription from the policy (exception handling)
az policy exemption create \
  --name allow-public-storage-sandbox \
  --policy-assignment $(az policy assignment show --name deny-public-storage --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/production --query id -o tsv) \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sandboxSubId> \
  --expires-on '2026-12-31'

# Check effective policy assignments on a resource group (inherited + direct)
az policy assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG \
  --include-parent \
  --query "[].{name:name, displayName:displayName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

# Verify compliance state of resources in a scope after policy assignment
az policy state summarize \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails[?complianceState=="NonCompliant"].{resource:resourceId, count:count}'

A ReadOnly lock on a resource group prevents creating new resources, modifying existing ones, or deleting them — even for users with Owner role. Locks are checked after RBAC: even an Owner cannot bypass a lock without first removing it (which itself is an audited control-plane operation).
Enterprise Scale: Azure Landing Zone Subscription Design

At enterprise scale, the management group and subscription hierarchy follows the Azure Landing Zone (ALZ) pattern. ALZ separates platform infrastructure (identity, connectivity, management) from application workloads, and separates production-connected workloads from internet-facing and sandbox environments. Each functional area gets its own subscription — not because subscriptions are security boundaries between teams, but because subscriptions provide natural cost allocation, quota, and policy isolation.

Connectivity (Hub VNet, ExpressRoute, VPN, Azure Firewall), Identity (Azure AD DS, Azure DNS private resolvers), and Management (Log Analytics workspace, Automation Account, Microsoft Defender for Cloud). These are shared services used by application subscriptions.
application subscriptions connected to the hub VNet via VNet peering. Subject to stricter policies than sandbox (allowed regions, mandatory tags, denied public storage, required diagnostic settings).
internet-facing workloads not connected to the hub. Suitable for public-facing web applications that don't need on-premises connectivity.
developer experimentation with permissive policies and a monthly budget cap. Resources auto-expire or are cleaned up by automation.

# Azure Landing Zone pattern: separate subscriptions per function
# This is the Microsoft-recommended enterprise scale pattern

# Management Groups structure:
# Root MG
#  Platform MG
#    Identity sub (Azure AD DS, DNS)
#    Connectivity sub (Hub VNet, ExpressRoute, VPN, Azure Firewall)
#    Management sub (Log Analytics, Defender for Cloud, Automation)
#  Landing Zones MG
#    Corp MG (connected to hub)
#      Finance sub
#      HR sub
#    Online MG (internet-facing, no hub connection)
#      eCommerce sub
#  Sandbox MG
#    Developer sandboxes (permissive policies, budget caps)
#  Decommissioned MG
#    Subscriptions being retired (budget = 0, no new resources)

# Create the Platform management group
az account management-group create \
  --name Platform \
  --display-name 'Platform' \
  --parent Root

# Create connectivity subscription (done in EA portal or billing API)
# Then move it to the Platform MG
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name Platform \
  --subscription <connectivitySubId>

# Apply allowed-locations policy at Landing Zones MG (restrict to approved regions)
az policy assignment create \
  --name allowed-regions \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c' \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/LandingZones \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations": {"value": ["eastus", "westus2", "eastus2"]}}'

Cost is always billed at the subscription level — all resources in a subscription aggregate to a single billing unit. However, Azure Cost Management allows viewing and filtering costs by resource group, resource, tag, and service type within a subscription. Budgets can be set at the subscription or resource group scope with alert thresholds (e.g., 80% and 100% of budget triggers email notifications). Effective cost governance requires a tagging strategy: every resource should be tagged with CostCenter, Environment, Owner, and Application, so costs can be attributed across organizational boundaries.

# Cost management at each scope level

# View cost breakdown by resource group (subscription scope)
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date '2026-06-01' \
  --end-date '2026-06-30' \
  --query "[].{service:instanceName, rg:resourceGroup, cost:pretaxCost, currency:currency}" \
  --output table

# Create budget at resource group scope with 80% and 100% alerts
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name rg-monthly-budget \
  --category Cost \
  --amount 5000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2026-07-01 \
  --end-date 2027-06-30 \
  --resource-group myRG-finance-prod \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["finance@contoso.com"]},{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":100,"contactEmails":["finance@contoso.com","cto@contoso.com"]}]'

# View cost by tag (requires tagging strategy at resource level)
# Azure Cost Management → Cost Analysis → Group by: Tag → CostCenter

# Export costs to storage account for custom analysis
az consumption export create \
  --account-name myBillingAccount \
  --billing-account-name myBillingAccount \
  --export-name monthly-cost-export \
  --type ActualCost \
  --recurrence Monthly \
  --storage-account-id $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myCostStorage --query id -o tsv) \
  --storage-container cost-reports \
  --storage-directory monthly

Each subscription has per-region quotas for compute cores, storage accounts, public IP addresses, VNets, and other resource types. These quotas are per-subscription-per-region and are the practical reason large workloads are spread across multiple subscriptions. A single subscription is limited to 980 resource groups, 800 deployments per resource group history, and region-specific vCPU quotas (default is 20 per VM family in most regions). Azure Resource Graph allows querying resources across subscriptions and management groups without switching subscription context.

# Subscription quotas: each subscription has per-region limits

# Check VM core quota in East US
az vm list-usage \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[?starts_with(name.value,'cores') || starts_with(name.value,'Standard')].{name:name.localizedValue, used:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# Request quota increase (via Azure portal or support API)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name 'Increase vCPU quota East US' \
  --title 'Request vCPU quota increase for East US' \
  --description 'Need 100 additional vCPUs in East US for production workload expansion' \
  --problem-classification '/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/quota/problemClassifications/cores' \
  --severity moderate \
  --contact-first-name John \
  --contact-last-name Doe \
  --contact-email admin@contoso.com \
  --contact-country US \
  --contact-preferred-time-zone 'UTC'

# Check current Azure Resource Graph for resource counts across subscriptions
az graph query \
  --graph-query 'resources | summarize count() by subscriptionId, type | order by count_ desc' \
  --first 20

# Resource Graph: find all VMs across all subscriptions in a management group
az graph query \
  --graph-query 'resources | where type == "microsoft.compute/virtualmachines" | project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, location, properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize' \
  --management-groups production \
  --output table
Tags can be applied at subscription, resource group, and resource levels. Tags do not inherit downward by default, but Azure Policy can enforce tag inheritance automatically.

# Apply a tag to a resource group
az group update \
  --name rg-prod-eastus \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCenter=12345 Owner=platform-team

# Apply a tag to a subscription
az tag create \
  --resource-id /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --tags Environment=Production BillingCode=ABC

# Enforce tag inheritance via Azure Policy
# Built-in policy: 'Inherit a tag from the resource group if missing'
az policy assignment create \
  --name inherit-env-tag \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/ea3f2387-9b95-492a-a190-fcdc54f7b070 \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "Environment"}}'

# List all resources with a specific tag value
az resource list --tag Environment=Production --query '[].{Name:name, Type:type, RG:resourceGroup}' -o table

# Audit resources missing a required tag
az resource list --query "[?tags.CostCenter==null].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup}" -o table
Best practice: Use Azure Policy with 'Deny' or 'Append' effects to enforce mandatory tags at subscription or management group scope. This catches non-compliant resources before they are created.

Resources can be moved between resource groups or subscriptions after creation, subject to resource-type restrictions. Moving does not re-create the resource — it updates the Azure Resource Manager registration.

# Validate that resources can be moved before attempting
az resource invoke-action \
  --action validateMoveResources \
  --ids /subscriptions/<src-sub>/resourceGroups/<src-rg> \
  --request-body '{"resources": ["/subscriptions/<src-sub>/resourceGroups/<src-rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm1"], "targetResourceGroup": "/subscriptions/<dest-sub>/resourceGroups/<dest-rg>"}'

# Move a VM to a different resource group
az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-archive-eastus \
  --ids /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm1

# Move an entire resource group's resources to another subscription
# Step 1: get resource IDs to move
RESOURCE_IDS=$(az resource list --resource-group rg-old --query '[].id' -o tsv | tr '
' ' ')

# Step 2: move them
az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-new \
  --destination-subscription-id <dest-sub-id> \
  --ids $RESOURCE_IDS

# Note: some resource types cannot be moved (e.g. Azure AD resources, Azure Backup vaults with items)
Move all apps in plan together
Choosing the right assignment scope for RBAC roles determines both security posture and administrative overhead. The principle of least privilege means assigning at the narrowest scope needed.

# --- Platform Team: MG-level broad read + write restricted ---
# Grant Reader at root MG (all subscriptions get read access)
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee platform-svc-principal@tenant.com \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/root

# Grant Contributor only on the Landing Zone MG
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee platform-svc-principal@tenant.com \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/landingzones

# --- App Team: subscription-scoped Contributor with deny overrides ---
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee app-team-group-id \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>

# Deny delete on production resource group (prevents accidental destroy)
az lock create \
  --name no-delete-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group rg-prod-eastus

# --- DBA Team: resource-group scoped role for SQL only ---
az role assignment create \
  --role 'SQL DB Contributor' \
  --assignee dba-team-group-id \
  --resource-group rg-data-eastus

# --- Audit: who has access at each scope level? ---
# List all role assignments at a management group
az role assignment list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/landingzones \
  --include-inherited --query '[].{Principal:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' -o table

# List effective permissions for a user at a resource group
az role assignment list \
  --assignee user@example.com \
  --resource-group rg-prod-eastus \
  --include-inherited -o table

Azure's management scope hierarchy has four levels: Management Groups (governance containers for subscriptions), Subscriptions (billing and trust boundaries), Resource Groups (lifecycle containers for resources), and Resources (individual services). Policies and RBAC role assignments assigned at any scope are inherited by all child scopes automatically. Management groups enable applying consistent security and compliance policies across an entire organization with a single policy assignment. Subscriptions are the billing unit — separate subscriptions are the standard pattern for isolating environments, business units, and applications. Resource groups are the deployment and lifecycle unit — deleting a resource group deletes all resources inside. Resource locks (CanNotDelete, ReadOnly) are enforced at the resource group or resource level and override RBAC — even an Owner cannot delete a locked resource without first removing the lock. Azure Policy exemptions allow specific child scopes to opt out of inherited policies with an expiry date.
